codeSnapshot.label: rmsprop.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Linear(10, 1)

# PyTorch RMSProp
optimizer = torch.optim.RMSprop(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=0.001,
    alpha=0.99,     # decay factor (Оі) for squared gradient EMA
    eps=1e-8,       # numerical stability
    weight_decay=0, # L2 regularisation
    momentum=0,     # optional momentum term
    centered=False  # if True: normalise by centered variance
)

# Manual implementation
class RMSProp:
    def __init__(self, params, lr=1e-3, alpha=0.99, eps=1e-8):
        self.params = list(params)
        self.lr, self.alpha, self.eps = lr, alpha, eps
        self.v = [torch.zeros_like(p) for p in self.params]  # EMA of gВІ

    def step(self):
        for p, v in zip(self.params, self.v):
            if p.grad is None: continue
            v.mul_(self.alpha).addcmul_(p.grad, p.grad, value=1-self.alpha)
            # v = Оұ*v + (1-Оұ)*gВІ
            p.data.sub_(self.lr * p.grad / (v.sqrt() + self.eps))

# Centered RMSProp: normalise by var(g) instead of E[gВІ]
# Ој_t = Оұ*Ој_{t-1} + (1-Оұ)*g_t
# v_t = Оұ*v_{t-1} + (1-Оұ)*g_tВІ
# update: g / sqrt(v_t - Ој_tВІ + Оө)
optimizer_centered = torch.optim.RMSprop(
    model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, alpha=0.99, centered=True
)

eli3: RMSProp (Root Mean Square Propagation) solves AdaGrad's dying learning rate problem. Instead of accumulating all past squared gradients, it uses an exponential moving average вҖ” recent gradients matter more, old ones fade away.

Think of it as a rolling average of how 'noisy' each parameter's gradient has been recently. If a parameter has had consistently large gradients, its effective learning rate stays small. If gradients suddenly become smaller (you're near a good value), the effective learning rate recovers, unlike AdaGrad where it only ever shrinks.
RMSProp was invented by Geoff Hinton in a Coursera lecture in 2012 вҖ” it was never formally published! It's the key building block of Adam: Adam = RMSProp + momentum + bias correction.

whenWhy.title: Recurrent neural networks and non-stationary objectives
whenWhy.body: RMSProp was originally proposed for training RNNs on non-stationary problems (Hinton, 2012 Coursera lecture). RNN gradients vary wildly across timesteps вҖ” AdaGrad's monotone decay causes learning to stop before the model converges. RMSProp's exponential forgetting adapts to the current gradient scale, keeping the learning rate useful throughout training.

whenWhy.title: Reinforcement learning (policy gradient methods)
whenWhy.body: In RL, the gradient signal is noisy and non-stationary (the reward function changes as the policy improves). OpenAI's original PPO and A3C implementations used RMSProp. The adaptive scaling helps stabilise training when reward variance is high. Many modern RL implementations have since switched to Adam or AdamW.

whenWhy.title: When Adam is unavailable (memory constraints)
whenWhy.body: Adam requires two moment buffers (m and v) per parameter = 3Г— model memory including weights. RMSProp requires only one buffer (v) = 2Г— model memory. For very large models or limited-memory settings, RMSProp is a lower-memory alternative with most of Adam's adaptive learning rate benefit (without the first moment / momentum component).

deepDiveIntro: RMSProp (Hinton, 2012, unpublished): vвӮң = ОіvвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + (1вҲ’Оі)gвӮңВІ; wвӮң = wвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ вҲ’ (О·/вҲҡ(vвӮң+Оө)) gвӮң. The exponential moving average vвӮң estimates E[gВІ] near time t with memory Оі. At steady state with stationary gradients, vвӮң вҶ’ E[gВІ]/(1вҲ’Оі) ... actually: since vвӮң = ОіvвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + (1вҲ’Оі)gвӮңВІ, at stationarity vвӮң вүҲ E[gвӮңВІ] (the (1вҲ’Оі) and the geometric series normalise correctly). The effective learning rate stabilises at О·/вҲҡE[gВІ] вҖ” unlike AdaGrad's monotone decay. [1]

Centered RMSProp (Graves, 2013): also subtracts the squared mean: ОјвӮң = ОіОјвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + (1вҲ’Оі)gвӮң; vвӮң = ОіvвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + (1вҲ’Оі)gвӮңВІ; wвӮң = wвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ вҲ’ О·В·gвӮң/вҲҡ(vвӮң вҲ’ ОјвӮңВІ + Оө). The denominator вҲҡ(vвӮң вҲ’ ОјвӮңВІ) estimates the standard deviation of gвӮң (not RMS). This normalises the gradient by its variance rather than its second moment вҖ” a more principled normalisation when the mean gradient is nonzero. [2]

deepDive.heading: Update Rule
deepDive.body: vвӮң = ОіВ·vвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + (1вҲ’Оі)В·gвӮңВІ. wвӮң = wвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ вҲ’ О·В·gвӮң / (вҲҡvвӮң + Оө). Effective lr for parameter i: О·/вҲҡvбөў,вӮң. Unlike AdaGrad, vвӮң is bounded вҖ” it doesn't grow forever. For stationary gвӮң with E[gвӮңВІ] = ПғВІ, vвӮң converges to ПғВІ (geometric series sum: ОЈвӮ– ОібөҸ(1вҲ’Оі)ПғВІ = ПғВІ). So effective lr stabilises at О·/Пғ. Typical Оі = 0.99 вҶ’ 100-step memory; Оі = 0.9 вҶ’ 10-step memory. [1]
deepDive.formula: vвӮң = ОіvвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + (1вҲ’Оі)gвӮңВІ;  wвӮң = wвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ вҲ’ О·В·gвӮң/вҲҡ(vвӮң+Оө)

deepDive.heading: RMSProp vs AdaGrad: Dying Learning Rate
deepDive.body: AdaGrad accumulates: GвӮң = ОЈвӮ–вӮҢвӮҖбө— gвӮ–ВІ вҶ’ вҲһ. Effective lr вҶ’ 0. RMSProp: vвӮң = ОіvвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + (1вҲ’Оі)gвӮңВІ. Weight of step tвҲ’k gradient: (1вҲ’Оі)ОібөҸ вҶ’ 0 as k вҶ’ вҲһ. Only recent gradients matter. If gradients decrease (near minimum), vвӮң decreases вҶ’ effective lr increases. This non-monotone learning rate is crucial for long training runs. The half-life of a gradient is log(0.5)/log(Оі) steps: вүҲ 69 steps for Оі=0.99. [1]

deepDive.heading: RMSProp + Momentum
deepDive.body: PyTorch RMSProp supports optional momentum: mвӮң = ПҒВ·mвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + gвӮң/вҲҡ(vвӮң+Оө); wвӮң = wвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ вҲ’ О·В·mвӮң. Adding momentum to RMSProp gets close to Adam but without bias correction. The main difference from Adam: (1) no first moment (momentum applied to the already-normalised gradient, not the raw gradient); (2) no bias correction for v (matters in first ~1/(1вҲ’Оі) steps). Empirically, RMSProp + momentum вүҲ Adam without bias correction on most tasks. [1]

takeaways: vвӮң = ОіvвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ + (1вҲ’Оі)gвӮңВІ; wвӮң = wвӮңвӮӢвӮҒ вҲ’ О·В·gвӮң/вҲҡ(vвӮң+Оө) вҖ” EMA fixes AdaGrad's dying lr. [1]
vвӮң converges to E[gвӮңВІ] at stationarity вҖ” effective lr stabilises at О·/вҲҡE[gВІ]. [1]
Centered RMSProp normalises by gradient std (вҲҡ(vвҲ’ОјВІ)) вҖ” more principled for non-zero mean. [2]
Adam = RMSProp + momentum (first moment) + bias correction. [1]
Lower memory than Adam (1 buffer vs 2) вҖ” useful for large models with tight memory budgets. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the effect of Оі (alpha) in RMSProp and how should it be tuned?
edgeCases.a: Оі controls the memory length of the second moment estimate. The half-life of past gradients is log(0.5)/log(Оі) вүҲ 69/(1вҲ’Оі) steps. Оі=0.9: 69-step memory вҖ” adapts quickly to changing gradient scales. Оі=0.99: 690-step memory вҖ” smooth, stable estimate. Оі=0.999: 6900-step memory вҖ” very slow adaptation. For non-stationary problems (RL, RNNs), use Оі=0.9 for fast adaptation. For stable supervised learning, Оі=0.99вҖ“0.999 gives better gradient scale estimates. Note: Adam uses ОІвӮӮ=0.999 for the same second moment.

edgeCases.q: Why is RMSProp not formally published, and does that affect trust in it?
edgeCases.a: Hinton introduced RMSProp in Lecture 6 of his Coursera 'Neural Networks for Machine Learning' (2012). There is no paper вҖ” just a 2-slide derivation. Despite this, RMSProp has been extensively validated empirically: Graves (2013) used it for handwriting generation RNNs; DeepMind used it in the original DQN; TensorFlow and PyTorch both implement it. The lack of a paper with theoretical guarantees is worth noting вҖ” RMSProp has no general convergence proof for non-convex problems, unlike Adam (which has asymptotic guarantees under mild assumptions).

sources.label: Hinton, G. (2012) вҖ” Neural Networks for Machine Learning, Lecture 6e (Coursera)
Graves, A. (2013) вҖ” Generating Sequences With Recurrent Neural Networks arXiv:1308.0850
